_comment: Top-level overview content. lede = opening paragraph. callouts = 4 boxes. key_patterns = enumerated descriptive findings. observed_patterns = the section formerly called 'best_practices' — descriptive, not prescriptive. age_analysis is its own block. Numbers come from scripts/build.py computations; rerun build after adding pitchers and refresh prose if conclusions shift. Round-4 expansion: 24→38 pitchers, 11→13 orgs (added DET, BOS), plus a 30-org 60+ IP MiLB league baseline (2023/2024/2025) that drives the per-org-page league-position chips.
lede: A descriptive study of how MLB organizations manage young, valued starting pitchers during their first full affiliated MiLB season. Surfaces visible in-game starter usage — pitch counts, rest, low-IP outings, build-up shape, response to promotions — derived from game logs only. Currently covers 38 pitchers across 13 organizations, 2022-2025 seasons (Jobe's 2022 included for DET time-series depth), ages 18-23, first full pro debuts through AAA. The sample is intentionally focused on arms that threw enough starts for management patterns to be visible; survivorship bias is a feature of the design, not a flaw. Each org page also surfaces the org's position within the full 30-org 2025 MiLB 60+ IP population (mean P/start, mean IP, mean GS, mean Vel4S, ranks 1-30) so the small-n featured-pitcher signal can be checked against the org-wide pattern. Not a measure of total throwing workload (bullpens / catch / live BP are not in the CSVs) and not an injury prediction tool.
inclusion_criteria: Featured-pitcher inclusion: a 'first full affiliated MiLB season' with enough starts to read management patterns (typically ≥17 GS or ≥80 IP). Round 4 added 14 arms; 4 marginal-fit candidates (Dishmey, Dubanewicz, Randall, Gough) were excluded entirely rather than carry asterisks. Jackson Jobe's 2022 season is included to give DET time-series depth — flagged as the earliest year in the dataset. League baseline reference: 884 pitcher-seasons across the 2023, 2024, and 2025 'MiLB pitchers age 18-22 with 60+ IP' files, all 30 orgs. League data is aggregated season totals (one row per pitcher-season) — no per-game detail in those files, so league-context comparisons are season-level only (IP, GS, P/start, K%, Strike%, Vel4S).

callouts.headline: Four pitchers' seasons sat almost entirely inside the 0.8–1.3 ACWR band.
callouts.body: Ford (SEA), White (MIA), Caminiti (ATL), and Santucci (NYM) spent 96-100% of qualifying starts inside the 0.8-1.3 reference band. Different orgs, ages, and build-up paths — read the four together as case studies in what unbothered usage looked like in this cohort, not a target other arms should hit. The Round-4 sample expansion did not add new arms at that 96-100% level; the cleanest tier is still the same four.

callouts.headline: Highest-IP pitchers who finished the season healthy: Hackenberg, Ford, Santucci.
callouts.body: Three pitchers who carried the most innings while staying in-band on ACWR and avoiding in-season injury flags: Hackenberg (ATL, 129 IP, 91% in-band, 3 levels), Ford (SEA, 125 IP, 100% in-band), Santucci (NYM, 122 IP, 96% in-band). Messick (CLE, 138 IP) also stayed healthy but carried two ACWR spikes and 1.88 max — high volume with a less-smooth ratio profile. Hess (NYY, 108 IP, 90% in-band) matched the metrics yet still ended up with a 27-day mid-season gap and injury history — clean visible workload data does not guarantee a clean season, because the CSV does not see bullpens or undisclosed issues. None of the 14 Round-4 additions cleared the high-IP-and-clean-ACWR bar; the new arms either ran lower IP totals (Paez 84, Sears 91, Elissalt 86) or higher ACWR profiles (DET sample averages 1.57 max ACWR).

callouts.headline: Age tracks pitch-count ceiling — and the 18-19 / 20-21 split is tighter than the Round-3 sample suggested.
callouts.body: Avg max P scales with age in this 38-pitcher cohort: 18-19yo 83P, 20-21yo 89P, 22+ 94P. In-band ACWR % is no longer monotonic and no longer cleanly U-shaped: 80% at 18-19 (down from 85% at n=9), 78% at 20-21 (up from 76% at n=7), 83% at 22+ (unchanged at n=8). The 'noisy middle' framing softened with the expanded sample — 18-19 and 20-21 now read more similarly than they did at smaller n. Three of the five pitchers with an ACWR > 1.5 still sit in the 20-21 group. With still-modest per-group n (15/15/8), treat as descriptive about the cohort, not a generalization about all minor-league pitchers.

callouts.headline: Visible workload is necessary context, not sufficient evidence.
callouts.body: Knoth (offseason TJ), Meyer (2024 nagging issue), Nichols (three IL stints in 2024), Hess (27-day mid-season gap) — none of these stand out in pitch count or ACWR alone. Extended rest gaps (>15 days) coincide with reported injuries, but for most cases the gap IS the IL stint — that is confirmatory, not predictive. ACWR spikes also did not reliably precede injuries here: of the pitchers with a spike > 1.5, only Nichols had an injury pattern that overlapped the spike. Cijntje's three spikes all landed during his high-frequency piggyback phase where the 3-start chronic baseline sat in the 30-50P range — small absolute pitch-count increases register as large ratios when the denominator is that low, so the spikes are a metric artifact, not a workload overload signal. We surface workload visibility because that is what the CSV shows; bullpens, side work, mechanics, and perceived effort are not in scope here.

observed_patterns.title: Patterns observed in the cleanest seasons in this cohort
observed_patterns.intro: Of the pitchers who covered 95+ IP, six combined that volume with a high in-band ACWR rate (≥80%, max ≤1.4, no spikes > 1.5) and finished the season without an in-season injury flag: Hackenberg (ATL), Ford (SEA), Santucci (NYM), McLean (NYM), Baumann (ATL), White (MIA). The Round-4 expansion did not add to this 'cleanest' six — none of the 14 new arms cleared the 95+ IP / ≥80% sweet / ≤1.4 max-ACWR / no-injury bar. The patterns below are what we observed in those six seasons. We are not claiming these patterns caused the outcome — only describing what the visible data showed.

items.title: Consistent weekly rhythm
items.body: All six held 6-7 day rest windows 85%+ of the time. Few unexplained compressions or extensions. Ford's 7-day rhythm is the strictest in the dataset (20 of 22 rest gaps at exactly 7 days). Rotation integrity is a shared feature of these seasons — we cannot tell from the CSV whether it was the org's deliberate plan or a happy byproduct of how the schedule fell.

items.title: Pitch-count caps expanded in step with promotions
items.body: Hackenberg opened at 70P and ended at 97P across three levels. Ford opened at 75P and ended at 89P. Santucci opened at 66P and ended at 81P. Caps climbed 15-25P over the season in staircase fashion, each step coinciding with a level move — not one aggressive jump, not stuck at a plateau. The CSV cannot tell us whether the cap rose because of the promotion or whether the promotion happened because the pitcher had earned the higher cap; the two move together.

items.title: All six earned at least one promotion in season
items.body: Hackenberg cleared three levels; the other five made at least one level jump. We can see that promotions happened and that pitch counts / ACWR stayed in their reference bands through the transitions; we cannot see why the org made each call from the CSV alone. What the data does show: none of the six had a regression or IL stint that pushed promotion timing back.

items.title: Max ACWR under 1.4 across all six seasons
items.body: Hackenberg 1.17, Ford 1.19, Santucci 1.29, McLean 1.28, Baumann 1.35, White 1.12. None exceeded 1.4 max ACWR. Contrast with Messick (CLE workhorse, 138 IP) at 1.88 max with 2 spikes — he stayed healthy too, but his ratio profile was different. The takeaway is descriptive: high-IP and clean ACWR can coexist in this cohort; high-IP without clean ACWR is also possible.

items.title: No unexplained mid-season gaps in these six seasons
items.body: Every gap above 10 days in this group was tied to a level promotion (8-11 days typical) or the All-Star break extension. No mystery 10+ day gaps. Across the broader cohort, when a gap of 15+ days appears without a promotion or ASB explanation, it almost always coincides with an injury event in the public reporting. So the absence of unexplained gaps is part of what makes these six seasons readable as 'clean.'

items.title: Five of six are 20+ years old
items.body: The lone 19yo is White (MIA) — who opened 2024 already capped near 75P thanks to 2023 buildup. Older pitchers in this sample carried more IP without ACWR distortion. The 18-19 group can also run reasonable in-band % (80% on average across n=15) but does not carry enough total IP to enter this volume category, which is consistent with how orgs treat the youngest arms in the sample.

age_analysis.title: Age group patterns
age_analysis.intro: Across 38 pitchers, age tracks workload ceiling more closely than org or level does. The Round-3-era U-shape in in-band ACWR % (tight at 18-19 and 22+, loose at 20-21) softened under the expanded sample: 18-19 and 20-21 now sit within 2 points of each other (80% vs 78%). 22+ still has the cleanest ACWR profile (83%) and the highest IP. We are describing what the cohort showed, not generalizing to all minor-league pitchers.

groups.label: 18-19 years old
groups.takeaway: Tight caps (mostly 72-87P range; Jobe's 2022 99P max in High-A is the outlier as a 1st-overall draft prospect getting accelerated runway). Avg ~85 IP — most pitchers top out 70-100 innings. ACWR discipline (80% sweet) is solid but slightly below where the smaller Round-3 18-19 sample looked (85%); the new arms carry a few more spikes. Still the 'protect the young arm' zone where orgs are most conservative on average and most uniform — but with n=15 we now see the variance more clearly.

groups.label: 20-21 years old
groups.takeaway: The expanded middle. Workload climbs (avg 102 IP) and caps push to 89P avg. ACWR discipline at 78% — improved 2 points over the smaller Round-3 sample, no longer a clear 'noisy middle' relative to 18-19's 80%. Three of the five pitchers in the dataset with ACWR spikes above 1.5 still sit here (Cijntje 2.08, Gill Hill 1.77, Nichols 1.66). Orgs deploy this cohort with the widest variance in caps (Patick 75P → Hamm/Sears 91-92P) and the widest variance in IP outcomes (Ford 125 down to Hamm 73).

groups.label: 22+ years old
groups.takeaway: Workhorse zone. Avg 111 IP, 94P max — teams deploy them like legitimate starter prospects. ACWR discipline is the cohort-best at 83%. Three of the four highest-IP pitchers in the dataset sit here (Messick 138, Hackenberg 129, Santucci 122). But not everyone in this group is clean: Cunningham ran 94P max and ended up on shoulder IL; Hess ran 99P max and had a 27-day gap with prior injury history. No new 22+ arms in Round 4 — group composition unchanged.

conclusions: Pitch count ceiling is primarily age-gated, not level-gated. Clemmey at 19 in Low-A capped at 80P; Messick at 23 in AA pushed to 97P. Jobe at 19 in High-A pushed to 99P (1st-overall draft pedigree, accelerated track). Same general scaling, but with enough variance that age alone doesn't fully predict cap.

ACWR discipline by age is no longer cleanly U-shaped under n=38. Tightest at 22+ (83%), then 18-19 (80%), then 20-21 (78%) — closer to monotonic-with-noise than the Round-3 U-shape. The Round-3 'noisy middle' read was a small-sample artifact.

Health-disruption cases correlate with pushing at or above age-appropriate ceiling. Cunningham (22yo) was hitting 92P+ regularly before his shoulder IL; Meyer (19yo) was at 79-85P right before the nagging issues began; Nichols (21yo) was at 82-87P across short-rest stretches before his first IL stint.

The healthiest high-volume seasons (Hackenberg, Ford, Santucci, McLean, Baumann, White) stayed INSIDE age-appropriate caps while accumulating total IP through frequency and rhythm integrity — not per-start overload.

Extended rest gaps (>15 days) coincide with reported injuries more reliably than ACWR spikes do — but remember these are largely confirmatory (the gap IS the IL stint), not predictive. Most pitchers with gaps over 15 days have a reported or inferred injury context (Meccage, Meyer, Cunningham, Hess, Nichols); White's 20-day gap was the 2024 Futures Game plus rest. By contrast, of the pitchers with ACWR spikes above 1.5, only Nichols had an injury pattern overlapping the spike. Cijntje's three spikes were a metric artifact of a low-pitch-count piggyback phase (3-start chronic baseline at 30-50P makes ACWR mathematically volatile); Messick stayed healthy; Ziehl's came during a mid-season trade rebuild; Gill Hill had no formal injury.

org_rankings.title: Organization-level descriptive snapshot
org_rankings.intro: Mean share of starts in the 0.8–1.3 ACWR reference band across each org's sampled pitchers. n ranges 1-6 — directional only, not a 'team philosophy.' Pair every figure here with the league-position chips on each org's page (rank within the 30-org 2025 MiLB 60+ IP population) before drawing inferences; the small-sample featured-pitcher number can read tighter or looser than the org-wide pattern. NYY in particular: 81.7% sweet on n=2 looks middle-tier, but in the 30-org 2025 60+ IP population NYY ranks 11/30 in mean P/start (93.6) and 7/30 in mean IP — squarely middle-of-pack at the org-wide level, not 'tightly managed' as a Round-3 read briefly suggested.

orgs.note: Both Santucci and McLean ran clean high-volume seasons with AA promotions. Cleanest 2-pitcher sample. Org-wide: NYM ranks 14/30 in P/start (90.8 mean) — the featured pair sit cleaner than the org-wide pattern.

Three pitchers, all clean. Hackenberg's 3-level year is the exemplar. Best-supported clean-tier sample. Org-wide: ATL ranks 21/30 in P/start (82.8 mean) — the featured trio runs higher P/start than the org's broader 60+ IP population.
L Martinez (added Round 4) joins White's 100% sweet and Meyer's 75%. Org-wide: MIA ranks 8/30 in P/start (95.5 mean) and 13/30 in IP — top-third in pitch volume across the 60+ IP MiLB population.
Clemmey (single-pitcher sample, directional) maintained 80P cap and clean ACWR across both orgs post-trade.

Paez (n=1, Round 4 first BOS entry) ran a clean 83P cap as a 20yo international arm in Low-A. Org-wide: BOS ranks 2/30 in P/start (107.4 mean) — the broader org-wide pattern is dramatically higher pitch-volume than Paez's individual season suggests.

With Humphries (Round 4) and Hernandez (Round 4) added, CLE's avg sweet is essentially unchanged from Round 3. Age-calibrated protocol holds: youngest arms (Doughty 19, Humphries 19, Hernandez 18) get tight caps; Messick (23) is the workhorse. Org-wide: CLE ranks 18/30 in P/start (85.8 mean) but 3/30 in IP (99.3) — high IP volume distributed across more outings, lower per-start P.
Ferris (Round 4) joins Patick and Zazueta. Caps still tight relative to age (75-92P range). Org-wide: LAD ranks 19/30 in P/start (85.0 mean) — featured trio in line with org-wide P/start pattern.

Cunningham + Hess both ran 94-99P early in their first pro season and both had in-season disruptions (shoulder IL, 27-day gap). The Round-3 read was that these two represented a managed top-tier pattern; the league baseline tells a different story — NYY ranks 11/30 in mean P/start across the 30-org 2025 60+ IP population, comfortably middle-of-pack rather than a top-tier protector. The 2-pitcher featured sample is reading tighter than the org-wide pattern, not a managed-org signal.

Largest org sample (n=6 with Corniel, M Rodriguez, Torres added). Sweet% improved 5 points from the n=3 Round-3 read (75.7%→80.3%). Knoth's clean numbers preceded TJ — the alarming 'no warning' case persists. Meccage injury-disrupted. Org-wide: MIL ranks 16/30 in P/start (88.1 mean) and 15/30 in IP — squarely middle of the population.

Cijntje's ACWR-max figure is inflated by low-pitch-count piggyback phase. Ford's season is dataset-cleanest. Org-wide: SEA ranks 28/30 in P/start (74.4 mean) — among the lowest mean per-start workloads across the 60+ IP MiLB population, while the 3-pitcher featured sample carries a higher max (Cijntje, Sloan).

Ziehl single-pitcher sample (directional only) — 3 ACWR spikes in the post-trade rebuild phase. Mid-season trade workload discontinuity; the 'aggressive' label reflects the trade disruption more than an org style.

Sample grew to 5 with Urbina and S Suarez added (Round 4). Sweet% essentially unchanged from Round 3 (68.9% → 71.0%). Across the 5 sampled arms: Nichols injury-disrupted (3 IL stints), Gill Hill one ACWR spike, Harrison the only confirmed skipped-turn case. Org-wide: TB ranks 26/30 in P/start (78.5 mean) but 2/30 in IP (100.3) — TB's broader pattern is high IP via lots of shorter outings, which the featured sample reflects.

Lowest sweet% in the sample. New in Round 4 with 4 arms across 2022-2025 (Jobe '22, Sears '24, Hamm '24, Elissalt '25). The mixed-year window matters — Jobe's accelerated 1st-overall track and Hamm's truncated 73 IP year both pull the avg. DET is the project author's org; included transparently as a comparator. Org-wide context (which is more representative than n=4 across 4 different seasons): DET ranks 24/30 in P/start (79.2 mean) and 21/30 in IP across the 30-org 2025 60+ IP population — bottom-third in both, but consistent with the sampled pattern of moderate caps and shorter individual outings rather than an aggressive workload approach. The sweet% number reflects a mix of small-n variance and an org that runs lower pitch counts across the board.

population_resonances.title: Resonances with the broader 30-org picture
population_resonances.intro: The per-pitcher game-log analysis and a separate 30-org population study (all MiLB pitchers age 18-22 with 60+ IP, 2023-2025, analyzed independently via ChatGPT) were not designed together. The five findings below are places where the two independent lenses point in the same direction — or pull apart in ways worth naming. Neither dataset sees what the other sees: the per-pitcher analysis tracks within-season start-by-start rhythm; the population study tracks season-over-season IP growth and return rates across all 30 orgs. Where they agree, the signal is stronger. Where they diverge, both readings are real.

items.headline: Age 19→20 is the pivotal workload threshold — confirmed by both lenses.
items.body: Our per-pitcher data shows pitch-count ceilings scale with age: 18-19yo avg 83P max, 20-21yo avg 89P, 22+ avg 94P. The 30-org population dataset shows the same inflection in season-to-season IP volume: the biggest year-over-year IP jump for same-org repeaters occurs at age 19→20 (+19.9 IP avg, 83% of pitchers increasing), then tapers to +11.9 IP at 20→21, and flatlines to -1.5 IP at 21→22. Two independent datasets, different granularity, same answer: age 20 is where orgs open the tap — both per start and per season.

items.headline: TB reads as the most aggressive org from both directions.
items.body: Our per-pitcher ACWR analysis ranks TB as the lowest ACWR sweet% in the sampled orgs (71%) — multiple spikes (Nichols 3 IL stints, Gill Hill, Harrison). The 30-org population dataset independently identifies TB as the most aggressive same-org year-over-year IP escalator among orgs with 5+ repeat pairs (+18.5 IP avg). Two independent methods working on independent samples point at the same organization. That convergence strengthens the read on TB's deployment philosophy beyond what either dataset could say alone.

items.headline: MIA's high population return rate validates the 'sustainable management' read.
items.body: Our per-pitcher analysis designates MIA's White (100% ACWR sweet, zero short starts, zero injury disruption) as the gold-standard season in this dataset and places MIA in the top tier of sampled orgs. The 30-org population study shows MIA leads all 30 organizations in strict same-org return rate: 58.3% of their 60+ IP pitchers came back to the same org the following year and threw more innings. That's 7 of 12 qualifying pitcher-seasons — best in the population. The per-start discipline we see within seasons appears to translate into arms that can actually carry more volume the next year.

items.headline: NYY's 0% same-org return rate adds population context to the first-year aggressive deployment concern.
items.body: Our 2-pitcher NYY sample (Cunningham: 94-99P first pro year → shoulder IL; Hess: 94-99P → 27-day gap) is too small to draw org-level conclusions. The 30-org population dataset adds a broader frame: NYY had 0 of 9 qualifying pitchers return to the same org with more IP the following year — the lowest strict return rate of any org with a meaningful cohort. Trades, MLB promotions, and injuries all collapse into that 0/9 figure; we cannot separate them from this data. But paired with the visible workload signals from our per-pitcher analysis, the combination warrants monitoring rather than dismissal. Not a finding — a flag.

items.headline: DET tension: 'most aggressive' ACWR tier but 2nd-best population return rate.
items.body: Our per-pitcher ACWR analysis places DET in the 'most aggressive' sampled tier (63.8% sweet) and the 30-org dataset ranks DET 24/30 in per-start pitch count. Both suggest DET runs below-average workload rhythm. But the population dataset also shows DET with the 2nd-highest same-org return rate (56%, 5 of 9 pitchers came back with more IP) and 4th-lowest pitches-per-inning (15.91 — meaning DET pitchers are efficient when deployed). These aren't contradictory: the ACWR metric captures within-season rhythmic inconsistency, while the return rate captures multi-season outcomes. DET's low per-start pitch counts may allow sustainable volume accumulation across seasons even when individual-start ACWR runs noisier. Both readings are real; reading one without the other misses the picture.

key_patterns.title: Short-start re-entry sits near pre-short workload, not below it
key_patterns.body: Median next-start-as-% of pre-short across the full dataset stays near 95-97% across 56 qualifying short-start events (Round 4 added one event vs Round 3's 55). Most orgs re-enter within 5-15 points of the pre-short workload rather than dropping protection. Read the Short-starts tab for per-org detail — NYY's two 2025 draftees got the most tempered re-entries, NYY/CHW's Ziehl post-trade the most aggressive. Only one pitcher in the dataset has a confirmed skipped turn after a short start (Harrison, TB, around the All-Star break window).

key_patterns.title: Organization hard-cap styles cluster — within small samples, and the league baseline often tells a different story
key_patterns.body: LAD's tight 75P sample look (Patick, Zazueta) survives the addition of Ferris (92P max), so the org now reads as 75-92P range across n=3, not a uniform 75 ceiling. MIA expands to n=3 with L Martinez (87P) joining White (86P) and Meyer (87P) — tightly clustered. CLE expands to n=4 (Doughty 80, Messick 97, Humphries 75, Hernandez 87) — age-calibrated holds. SEA's caps still climb with both age and level. MIL across n=6 spans 80-94P. NYY's 94-99P sample suggested an aggressive first-year pattern at n=2; the league baseline puts NYY at rank 11/30 in mean P/start org-wide, so the featured-sample pattern is not org-representative. DET's n=4 sample (83-92P) lines up with DET ranking 24/30 in mean P/start across the 30-org 60+ IP population — the org genuinely runs lower per-start volume than most. Read these as starting hypotheses scoped by the league-position chips on each org page, not settled philosophies.

key_patterns.title: Multi-level promotions happen cleanly when data supports it
key_patterns.body: Hackenberg (ATL 2024) went Rome → Mississippi → Gwinnett — three levels in one season, 129 IP, no injury. Messick (CLE 2024) and Hess (NYY 2025) both went High-A → AA. McLean (NYM 2024) and Santucci (NYM 2025) both climbed High-A → AA with clean performance. Jobe (DET 2022) went High-A → Low-A as part of the 1st-overall accelerated runway with 99P caps. Each transition was handled with an 8-11 day gap. With 38 pitchers we now detect 25 promotions across 21 pitchers (Round 3: 21 across ~17).

key_patterns.title: Invisible injury signatures — watch for these patterns
key_patterns.body: Four cases in this dataset show 'pushing through an undiagnosed issue' signatures that wouldn't trigger ACWR alarms. (1) Meyer (MIA 2024): two 19-21 day gaps without formal IL placement. His returns were short in IP (1-2 IP outings) but the pitch counts actually held within the 85-105% re-entry band of pre-gap starts (6/27 return: 88.8% of pre-gap pitches; 7/24 return: 88.7%) — better read as 'limited innings on a tempered pitch count' rather than a broken workload protocol. The injury signature here is the IP-vs-pitch-count mismatch, not a pitch-count spike. (2) Nichols (TB 2024): three separate IL stints with low-volume outings in between — season capped at 71 IP. (3) Knoth (MIL 2024): completely clean workload pattern followed by offseason TJ — the scariest case, no visible signal. (4) Hess (NYY 2025): 27-day mid-season gap with injury history prior to the draft, ACWR looked fine throughout.

key_patterns.title: Aggressive first-year deployment — a pattern worth tracking, not yet a claim
key_patterns.body: Three first-pro-season cases in the dataset had both a high pitch-count ceiling and an in-season disruption: Cunningham (NYY 2025, 22yo college draftee) pushed to 92P+ in 5 of 7 pre-injury starts, then missed 65+ days with shoulder; Hess (NYY 2025, 22yo college draftee) ran 99P max in first pro season with a 27-day mid-season gap; Knoth (MIL 2024, 18yo prep) is the ambiguous case — chronic workload was building but nothing screamed warning, and his offseason TJ came after the CSV window. The Round-4 expansion did not add new cases that fit this pattern. Three pitchers (two from the same org) is too small to call the pattern causal. The honest framing: first-year deployment above 92P is worth flagging forward as a cohort to monitor, not presented as a settled org concern.

key_patterns.title: Extended rest gaps are confirmatory, not predictive — and ACWR spikes are noisy
key_patterns.body: Of the pitchers with a rest gap above 15 days, most had reported or inferred injury issues (Meccage, Meyer, Cunningham, Hess, Nichols). But read this as descriptive, not predictive — for most of those, the gap IS the IL stint. White's 20-day exception was the 2024 Futures Game plus a planned skip. The usable takeaway: when you see an unexplained gap of 15+ days in a pitcher's log, expect an injury in the context (even if no formal IL was reported). Meanwhile, of the pitchers with ACWR spikes above 1.5, only Nichols had an injury pattern overlapping the spike. Cijntje's three spikes are a metric artifact: they all landed during his high-frequency piggyback phase where the 3-start chronic baseline sat in the 30-50P range, and ACWR ratios go volatile when the denominator is low (a 15P → 45P jump reads as a 3.0 ACWR without meaning the workload is dangerous). Messick spiked twice as the CLE workhorse and stayed healthy; Ziehl's spike came in the post-trade rebuild; Gill Hill had one spike with no formal injury. ACWR spikes without context are noisy — and ACWR as a tool assumes a relatively stable chronic baseline, so it breaks down when a pitcher is deployed at highly variable per-outing volumes.

key_patterns.title: All-Star break rest splits into two patterns — outcomes vary, we can't attribute them
key_patterns.body: Minimal-disruption crowd (+2-3 days): Santucci, Meccage, Patick, Sloan, Birchard — shifted rotation and came back quickly. Extended-rest crowd (+4-5 days): Ford, Caminiti, Doughty, Harrison, White — got a full week off. Comeback-start outcomes were mixed in both groups (Ford came back 88P, Santucci came back 1.2 IP / 46P) but with two data points in each bucket we can't claim the break length drove the outcome — opponent quality, random variation, and other scheduling factors all overlap. The descriptive fact is the rest split; the causal story isn't in the CSV.

key_patterns.title: League-baseline context — a useful corrective on small-n featured-sample reads
key_patterns.body: Each org page now carries a 4-chip ribbon showing where that org ranks across the 30-org 2025 MiLB 60+ IP population (884 pitcher-seasons across 2023/2024/2025) for mean P/start, mean IP, mean GS, and mean Vel4S. The most informative takeaways: NYY at 11/30 in P/start is middle-of-pack, not 'tightly managed' as a 2-pitcher featured sample suggested. BOS at 2/30 in P/start runs much higher pitch volumes org-wide than Paez's single-pitcher sample shows. CLE at 18/30 P/start but 3/30 IP confirms the 'lots of shorter outings, lots of total IP' pattern visible in the featured sample. DET at 24/30 P/start lines up with the sampled DET pattern of moderate caps. SEA at 28/30 P/start is among the lowest per-start across MiLB. League data is season-aggregated (no per-game detail), so comparisons are season-level — but they bound the small-sample featured-pitcher reads with broader-population context.
